transcript.text: Hey, greetings, Andi.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
Good.

Nice to meet you.
It's a pleasure for me to meet you too.

Yep.
That is correct.
Awesome.
Well, I'll start by giving you a little bit of context.
Thanks.
About me, the way maybe that I'm thinking about this role, and then, we can jump into your experience and everything else.
So, I manage customer operations for GrowthX, which can mean a lot of things at a lot of companies.
For us, it means I'm helping manage the team of directors that owns all of our account interactions.

And so, the way our pods are structured, I don't know how much context you have, but we have directors, then we have managing editors, who you may, that's probably who you're interacting with the most.
So, like Vivek and Jess, I think you're talking with a lot.
And then, we have a content manager, so those are the people that are using GrowthX's workflows to actually generate the articles that you're helping us to get online.
I think you're pretty familiar with the process just from what you've been doing, but this role will probably evolve as we grow and also as we get more engineering capacity.
So I know that you had a pretty technical exercise that you were asked to do.
I also saw that you have some pretty technical experience, at least in my world, because I've always been in sales and account management.
So I'll give you that.
And then this role kind of is going to sit in between those two, right?
So our engineers are building out the workflows, specifically, that is going to generate the content.
They're also, in a lot of instances right now, building API connections directly into the publishers so that we can.
Push content directly into the publishers, but we have 36 clients now.
That's always growing, and I think there's going to be a lot of kind of technical account management and support work, even as we're building those connections.
So today, we don't have connections into pretty much any of our publishers, as I think you're aware.

But going forward, even as we start to build those connections, you will kind of be the person that helps troubleshoot when a workflow stops working with a publisher, helps make updates, and just really is responsible for being that last mile human in the loop before something goes live.
So, like, you know, keeping an eye on things like formatting and making sure that things don't get messed up as they're going through those pipelines.
So, that's high level.
Well, how I'm thinking about this role, publishing, as you're probably aware, it takes up a ton of the time of the team that's like generating the content, and we want to be able to do both at scale.
So you've probably also seen that there are a lot of different publishing systems that we're working in.
So, and that will probably continue to change.
I don't know if any of that had been, like, if all of this is old news, but I just thought I'd give you my sense of it first.

I believe that was slightly different from what I was initially proved that was, like, initially what I was conveyed was, I would be the person that would be developing the pipelines that takes the contents, like, obviously the pipeline that creates the content is already done.
My job would be to pick up that content and make a pipeline that can publish it to different CMS, like, like, scrappy or any other headless CMS.
So that is what I was conveyed initially, but from what you have given.

The information you have shared with me, it seems like I'll be the one that will be just reviewing what is done, like as a last person to review if the code is according to standards or if there is any possible edge case that can cause issue in the productions.
So engineering team would be responsible to create them, I'd be the one to test them and make sure they are robust and can last for the production.
So am I correct or is something missing in it?
No, I think, thank you for calling out those differences.
I think, I'm not going to say that you will never build out your own publishing pipeline.
I think this role does not sit in the engineering team.

And I know the engineering team is owning those pipelines, but I also think there's opportunity for you to, like, if that's something that you want to be doing down the line or like a skill that you want to develop, I don't think that's out of the realm of possibility.
For, we're a startup, we've been around for less than a year.
current we've objectives made Thank

perfect.

That was a success initially, that we had to stop the project for a time while.

Do you have an example of when you've done that in the past like when you didn't have any direction and you picked up work or like can you talk me through when you've done that?

Okay so a very practical example is the growthx plugin I mentioned earlier Jason just told me this is something we want to do we want to scrape the images the memos and clicks for that and that's what you have to do.
since Jason is more of a non-technical person.
In terms of that, I started with this one, and I came across a lot of pockets.
For certain points, usually we have to do that is to get an API for that.
out, Upwork will give you API, if request it, it will take up to one month to get the API back.
And then I knew, the goal is to solve it for other customers.
So if we pitch that product to other customers, they would have to request API2, they would have to wait for one month, not a very practical solution.
I diverted the solution, moved to a more scraping side.
Where API is not required.
I used, I created a plugin that mimicked the human action, played with images, like clicked on the next, next, next.
Along the way, it kept on getting the images data, and blah, I was at the last.
And then I came to know, there is a restriction that I never knew in my life.
You cannot see an Upwork image after five minutes.
So there is a token with that, that expires after five minutes.
So all that Jason told me was to solve this problem, and no, I knew I had to do it.
So I came with that idea.

I thought instead of gathering all the images, and in the last processing it, then came to know, oh, scraping took more than five minutes, the images have already expired, and you cannot do anything with that.
I modified the approach slightly, I used a multi-threading approach, where as soon as I was getting an image, I was in a separate cycle, processing the images, and keep on gathering the data.
So when you reach on the last of the screen, you will have all the images already processed.

So Jason just gave me one task, I faced the problems, I came up with the solutions for that, and in the very last I informed Jason, this was the problems I faced, this is how I tackled them, and here is your end product.
